text:
<@UMVFWPZ36>
> I believe there is a fork in PLs between 1D and 2D (and more) notations.
Most PLs' user interface (what a programmer has access to) is the 1D text representation. Lisps also give access to the AST level. Forth and friends give access to an ASL level (L = list rather than T = tree). Spreadsheets are 2D only. There is a lot of space for experimentation, which I why I like your tree notation experiments, though I am pretty sure I haven't grasped its potential yet.

elements.text: I believe there is a fork in PLs between 1D and 2D (and more) notations.

text:
>  (text underline, font color, font size)
The two traps to avoid there are (1) accessibility issues (Daltonians etc.) and (2) changing user expectations. Syntax highlighting in text editors has created the expectation that text attributes have no semantics.

elements.text: (text underline, font color, font size)

text:
:wave: Hey folks! A friend pointed me towards this community a few months ago and I've been lurking since! Have slowly been making my way through some of the content (Whole Code Catalog and related) and still can't believe how much amazing stuff you all have gathered here. SO cool that this community exists! :sparkles:

Similarly to what I've seen in a couple of other introductions, I got hooked on the vision of a much better future after watching Inventing on Principle by Bret Victor. Spent some time investigating the space of "mobile apps for software engineers" for my bachelor thesis. Then worked at GitHub for a couple of years, mostly on and around the GitHub platform/API. Left that job to focus full time on building the <@U012ZSD9U65> Slack app (which remains my primary income source but has little to do with the future of coding).

Over the past year I've been thinking a lot about how to make software engineering much much faster. This culminated in me building <https://kolo.app|Kolo> which brings some liveness/runtime inspection/visualisation to Django + VSCode. Kolo launched two weeks ago in alpha, so definitely still under heavy development and I'm taking in all the feedback I can. Hoping to contribute to the  <#C0120A3L30R|two-minute-week> channel on occasion! :crossed_fingers:Oh and I’m based in London! :gb:

text: OK, here's a question I am being asked a lot but I have no ready answer but always trying to come up with answers on the fly: why do we need end user programming? I mean, I am totally convinced that democratizing coding is super important and will change the world. But when I talk to "normal people" they don't seem to feel the need. I always have trouble to come up with examples where creating and running code is useful, instead of just downloading an app or whatever.

elements.text: OK, here's a question I am being asked a lot but I have no ready answer but always trying to come up with answers on the fly: why do we need end user programming? I mean, I am totally convinced that democratizing coding is super important and will change the world. But when I talk to "normal people" they don't seem to feel the need. I always have trouble to come up with examples where creating and running code is useful, instead of just downloading an app or whatever.

text: One problem is that every opportunity for creating your own code that is high-value enough for enough people will be eventually covered by a bespoke app. This takes away a lot of possible examples.

elements.text: One problem is that every opportunity for creating your own code that is high-value enough for enough people will be eventually covered by a bespoke app. This takes away a lot of possible examples.

text: So what's the advantage of giving everyone the equivalent of a Python shell but much more user friendly?

elements.text: So what's the advantage of giving everyone the equivalent of a Python shell but much more user friendly?

text: (Again, I am convinced that this is important, but it is almost a faith-based conviction and not one rooted in actual user research)

elements.text: (Again, I am convinced that this is important, but it is almost a faith-based conviction and not one rooted in actual user research)

text:
One way I use is to ask about dissatisfactions within existing apps or between apps. It's pretty easy in my experience to get little things from anyone that they wish they could change, situations where two apps would interoperate.

No comment on how easy these use cases are to solve for. But my experience is that everyone has things they wish would change, even if nobody wants to learn anything to actually make a change. Which is natural and human nature. I hate the abstract idea of having to learn new things just to fix some minor annoyance, even if I'm constantly doing so in practice where the energy barriers are economic.

text:
End user programming in my mind is about changing the landscape of energy barriers.

(Any time somebody says something is a social rather than technical problem, it's really a social problem subject to a landscape of energy barriers hugely influenced by technical design choices.)

elements.text:

(Any time somebody says something is a social rather than technical problem, it's really a social problem subject to a landscape of energy barriers hugely influenced by technical design choices.)

text: So that's about taking existing apps and modifying that - I see that point, but wouldn't that assume that all these apps are available as open source and that they are created within the end-user programming system?

elements.text: So that's about taking existing apps and modifying that - I see that point, but wouldn't that assume that all these apps are available as open source and that they are created within the end-user programming system?

text:
Yeah, I tend to assume those prerequisites. A common definition for the term: changing the behavior of a system should be no more difficult than using the system. Which pre-assumes that there is an existing system.

I currently think of closed platforms like the App Store as antithetical to end-user programming. But I'm sure others here have wildly divergent views :slightly_smiling_face:

(I actually tried recently to capture the state space of ideas/projects/goals that are easy to conflate: <http://akkartik.name/post/neighborhood>. Additions most welcome.)

text:
OK, understood, and a very worthy goal.
I am more wondering about a path forward for end-user programming within the world as it is, where we have to share the space with closed source apps, and where the "end user development space" is probably just an app like any other.

text: :pray::skin-tone-2: The text shouldn't be flush to the left margin, it makes it harder to read because the eye fights the edge. Also, if you're designing for readability, the body copy should average between 12-14 words wide on each line. Wikipedia's default style sheet has long violated this latter norm, which makes it more difficult to read articles without an alternate stylesheet.

text:
Is Yahoo Pipes or ifttt an example of the sort of thing you're thinking about?

It sounds like your original question can be rephrased as people asking, "why do I need to use a new app to get the cool programmability features you're talking about? Why can't you meet me where I live, without me needing to change my habits?" Is this accurate?

To me it seems self-evident that if I can't modify some external thing (website written in obfuscated JS/webassembly, app, OS, piece of hardware) that limits how much I can interoperate with it to whatever its designers happened to take into account. Does that seem like giving up too easily?

text:
Ah, I see. I'm privileged to keep my windows wide enough that it doesn't go flush with the left margin.

As an experiment I just reduced the width of my website. In the past I found pages like <http://paulgraham.com/essay.html> too narrow, but now my site is the same width, and neither seems off. I'll keep this for a while and see how it feels.

More comments most welcome!

text: PG's essay has a width of 8-10, which is -- as you perceived -- narrower than is optimal. (I went through a phase of studying traditional book design when I started laying out text heavy web sites in the 90s, which had the negative side-effect of making me an effete snob about these things.)

text:
Many hurdles:
• A person being frustrated and not remotely having the concept that the problem app could, even in principle, be under their control.  
• Now imagining the app could "do that one thing" but not having a clear idea of how it would work.
• Having an idea of how the new feature would work, but having no idea how to make it work.
• Having an idea of how to make it work, and now at last we come to the end-user-programming problem of making it work be tractable.
Considering that even if rich invocation is possible, it's hard to cast the spell, "Hey Siri, set an alarm to go run the errand at 8:30am on Wednesday."

Or even if all the affordances seem to be in place,   looking at the iOS Shortcuts app right now, if there's, say subtle concurrency constraint, even though I'm a professional, there may be no hope.  In that case, why even bother?

text:
The easier software becomes to make, the wider the opportunities to create it, because the time investment makes sense. I once wrote a python script to combine photos into 2x2 combi photos because it was cheaper than getting them printed quarter size. I wouldn't have bothered either combining them manually, or doing it in assembly.

A huge number of work-hours are wasted operating computers manually at the moment.

Many, many businesses create (or comission) bespoke software that is only used by them. In my experience the process of building this software ("requirements gathering" **shudder**) is about 5% efficient at actually solving practitioner needs. All this stuff should be made by end users because it will actually result in better software.

text: I agree with all that, but I worry that 'efficiency' is an idea too close to 'delegate', which is anathema in some ways to this whole endeavor. Personally I prefer the argument about the _qualitatively different_ things that get made when everyone takes charge rather than a small group of engineers and PMs.
